paraphrase: Game of Thrones Season 7 confirmed that the father of Jon Snow is Rhaegar Targaryen.

At a tournament at Harrenhal, Rhaegar passed over his wife Elia Martell and crowned Lyanna Stark -- who was promised to marry Robert Baratheon -- as his 'queen of love and beauty,' angering the Baratheons, Starks, and Martells; the next year Rhaegar ran off with Lyanna, and Robert started a war to get her back.
King Robert's official account is that Rhaegar abducted and raped Lyanna.
Season 7 reveals that Robert's abduction/rape account is a 'lie,' and that Rhaegar and Lyanna were in love.
There are hints in the books that Lyanna rode in the Harrenhal tournament in disguise as a mystery knight, and that Rhaegar was sent to find her -- probably how they met.
Rhaegar and Lyanna fell in love and secretly got married.
Rhaegar and Lyanna ran away to a tower in Dorne to be together, until Rhaegar was forced to go to war against Robert.

spoken_quote: with Lyanna's name on his lips
paraphrase: Rhaegar died 'with Lyanna's name on his lips' at the war with Robert, while Lyanna died in childbirth.

Even if people had known the truth -- that Lyanna loved Rhaegar and went with him willingly -- the Baratheons and Starks would still have been furious, because this was not a society that let women choose who they loved; Lyanna was a political tool being used to build a Baratheon-Stark marriage alliance, and Rhaegar's taking her ruined those plans.
Rhaegar also insulted House Martell/Dorne by publicly rejecting his wife Elia in favor of Lyanna.

The political situation was especially dangerous because King Aerys Targaryen was by then well known to be mad -- violent, paranoid, obsessed with fire, and burning alive people he believed were his enemies; he was a terrible king many powerful people wanted gone.
Tywin Lannister had a grudge against Aerys and reportedly said he'd prefer Rhaegar as king.
The Baratheons, Starks, Arryns, and Tullys were forming a political alliance against Aerys; marrying Lyanna to Robert was part of that alliance.
Rhaegar himself was apparently plotting against his father -- the Tourney of Harrenhal is said to have begun as a plot so Rhaegar could conspire with the great houses to force Aerys into retirement.

By publicly crowning Lyanna, Rhaegar insulted the very families who could have been his allies against Aerys, throwing away his own political scheme; running off with Lyanna then set off the chain of events causing the war (the Starks came to King's Landing angry, Aerys killed them and demanded Robert's and Ned's heads).
Barristan Selmy says that no one truly knew Rhaegar -- he had a private, secretive side and a melancholy, 'a sense ... of doom,' connected to his birth at Summerhall.

spoken_quote: He was born in grief … and that shadow hung over him all his days
paraphrase: Rhaegar's great-grandfather King Aegon V brought the family to the Targaryen palace of Summerhall to celebrate Rhaegar's impending birth, but Aegon V's attempt at a fiery magic ritual to hatch dragon eggs went wrong and the fire killed him and much of the family; Rhaella gave birth to Rhaegar 'amongst the flames' during this tragedy, and 'he was born in grief ... and that shadow hung over him all his days.'
window: and that shadow hung over him all his days
verbatim: “. . . of doom. He was born in grief, my queen, and that shadow hung over him all his days.”

paraphrase: The Targaryens have historically been guided by visions and prophecy; they survived the Doom of Valyria because of visions of Rhaegar's ancestor Daenys the Dreamer.
Daeron and Daemon Blackfyre are shown having prophetic dreams in the Dunk and Egg novellas.
Rhaegar was born because of prophecy in the first place: his parents Aerys and Rhaella married because a witch said 'the prince that was promised' would be born of their line.

The prince that was promised, or Azor Ahai, is a hero prophesied to be reborn and save the world from darkness, generally read as meaning the fight against the White Walkers; the prophecy is vague and interpreted differently by different characters.

Melisandre believes Stannis Baratheon is Azor Ahai, but this interpretation 'doesn't work out'; others in-world and in fandom believe Daenerys is the prophesied hero instead -- rival candidates that compete with any Rhaegar-line identification.
As a boy, Rhaegar wasn't interested in playing with other children -- he just wanted to read books, until one day he read about Azor Ahai and came to believe he himself was the prophesied hero.

spoken_quote: It seems I must be a warrior
paraphrase: Young Rhaegar went to the master-at-arms and said he needed a sword: 'It seems I must be a warrior.'
verbatim: “As you wish,” said Whitebeard. “As a young boy, the Prince of Dragonstone was bookish to a fault. He was reading so early that men said Queen Rhaella must have swallowed some books and a candle whilst he was in her womb. Rhaegar took no interest in the play of other children. The maesters were awed by his wits, but his father’s knights would jest sourly that Baelor the Blessed had been born again. Until one day Prince Rhaegar found something in his scrolls that changed him. No one knows what it might have been, only that the boy suddenly appeared early one morning in the yard as the knights were donning their steel. He walked up to Ser Willem Darry, the master-at-arms, and said, ‘I will require sword and armor. It seems I must be a warrior.’”

paraphrase: Rhaegar discussed his belief in letters with his great-granduncle Maester Aemon, who agreed Rhaegar was Azor Ahai, reasoning that the prophecy says Azor Ahai will be reborn 'amidst smoke and salt' -- which they took to mean the smoke of the Summerhall fire and the salt of the tears shed there.

Because Azor Ahai is described as a 'warrior of fire' who will lead 'the fight against the dark,' Rhaegar trained and became a great fighter, shaping his life from a young age around fulfilling the prophecy.

Rhaegar's beliefs later changed: Maester Aemon says Rhaegar came to believe Azor Ahai was not himself but his son Aegon (the baby he had with Elia), because the prophecy says Azor Ahai will be born 'beneath a bleeding star,' and Rhaegar and Aemon believed this star was a comet seen the night Aegon was conceived.
verbatim: “Born amidst salt and smoke, beneath a bleeding star. I know the prophecy.” Marwyn turned his head and spat a gob of red phlegm onto the floor. “Not that I would trust it. Gorghan of Old Ghis once wrote that a prophecy is like a treacherous woman. She takes your member in her mouth, and you moan with the pleasure of it and think, how sweet, how fine, how good this is . . . and then her teeth snap shut and your moans turn to screams. That is the nature of prophecy, said Gorghan. Prophecy will bite your prick off every time.” He chewed a bit. “Still . . .”

spoken_quote: is the prince that was promised … There must be one more … The dragon has three heads
paraphrase: In a vision Daenerys has in Book 2, she sees Rhaegar, Elia, and their baby Aegon; Rhaegar says Aegon 'is the prince that was promised,' and 'There must be one more ... The dragon has three heads.'

The three-headed dragon on the Targaryen banner represents the three siblings who led House Targaryen three hundred years ago -- Aegon, Rhaenys, and Visenya; Daenerys notes these names connect to Rhaegar's own children, Aegon and Rhaenys.
This implies Rhaegar, saying 'there must be one more,' wanted a third child -- a daughter he may have intended to name Visenya -- to complete the dragon-with-three-heads set.

Elia was always 'frail and sickly,' and after giving birth to Aegon she could not bear any more children, so if Rhaegar believed 'there must be one more' he would need another woman to produce his third child -- which is where Lyanna Stark comes in.
verbatim: A bride for our bright prince. Jon Connington remembered Prince Rhaegar’s wedding all too well. Elia was never worthy of him. She was frail and sickly from the first, and childbirth only left her weaker. After the birth of Princess Rhaenys, her mother had been bedridden for half a year, and Prince Aegon’s birth had almost been the death of her. She would bear no more children, the maesters told Prince Rhaegar afterward.

paraphrase: It seems likely that part of the reason Rhaegar ran off with Lyanna and fathered Jon Snow was to have a third child fitting the three heads of the dragon; if he was originally planning for a daughter (Visenya), that didn't work out, but he may have changed his mind and decided this third child, Jon, would be Azor Ahai instead.
Rhaegar's/Aemon's prophecy discussion ties Azor Ahai to something called 'the song of ice and fire' -- the title of the book series.

Fan wordplay: Lyanna Stark of Winterfell is connected to ice, and Rhaegar of the Targaryen dragonlords is connected to fire, so a child of Lyanna and Rhaegar would be a 'song' -- or a 'son' -- of ice and fire.

spoken_quote: for a glimpse of Azor Ahai
paraphrase: There are hints Jon may be Azor Ahai: Melisandre prays 'for a glimpse of Azor Ahai' and her god shows her Jon; there's smoke, salt, and a bloody star in the scene where Jon dies; and Ned's promise to protect Jon makes him a 'prince' that was literally 'promised.'
verbatim: Yet now she could not even seem to find her king. I pray for a glimpse of Azor Ahai, and R’hllor shows me only Snow. “Devan,” she called, “a drink.” Her throat was raw and parched.

paraphrase: It might have been Lyanna who realized Jon was the true prince that was promised -- possibly reflected in the show by her naming him Aegon, reasoning 'what better name' for the prince that was promised.
It seems that in the end Rhaegar achieved his goal: his actions with Lyanna led to the birth of Jon, who may be the hero who will save the world.

Objection: does this really justify Rhaegar's actions? Couldn't he have found a way to father Jon without abandoning his family and causing a war that killed thousands? How could he be certain that taking Lyanna was the only way?

Objection: vague prophecy seems like a convenient excuse for Rhaegar to do things he probably wanted to do anyway -- ignoring difficult political realities, running away from his problems, and pursuing a much younger woman; Lyanna was just fifteen in the books, while Rhaegar was a twenty-something-year-old prince.
Rhaegar took Lyanna to a tower he called the 'tower of joy,' where the two of them stayed for about a year making 'prophecy babies' while the rest of the realm was at war.
verbatim: It would have to be his grandfather, for Jory’s father was buried far to the south. Martyn Cassel had perished with the rest. Ned had pulled the tower down afterward, and used its bloody stones to build eight cairns upon the ridge. It was said that Rhaegar had named that place the tower of joy, but for Ned it was a bitter memory. They had been seven against three, yet only two had lived to ride away; Eddard Stark himself and the little crannogman, Howland Reed. He did not think it omened well that he should dream that dream again after so many years.

paraphrase: Closing challenge to the theory: are these really the actions of a hero trying to save the world, or was Rhaegar 'a selfish idiot who killed thousands cause he was thinking with his dick'?
Part of the Azor Ahai prophecy is that the hero will wield a 'burning sword' called Lightbringer.

In Book 2, a story is told of how the original Lightbringer was forged: Azor Ahai tried three times -- the first sword broke when tempered in water, the second broke when tempered by stabbing the heart of a lion, but the third succeeded when he tempered it by stabbing the heart of his beloved wife, who cried out 'with anguish and ecstasy' and died, her strength passing into the steel to forge the true Lightbringer and save the world.
Some readers have noticed this forging story mirrors Rhaegar's own story: like Azor Ahai, he 'tried three times' to fulfil the prophecy.
First sword: Rhaegar believed he himself was Azor Ahai, but he died in the waters of the river Trident -- mirroring the first sword, which broke when tempered in water.

Second sword: Rhaegar believed his son Aegon was Azor Ahai, but Aegon was killed in the war by the men of Tywin Lannister, the Lion -- mirroring the second sword, which broke when tempered on a lion's heart.

Third sword/true Lightbringer: Rhaegar conceived a child with his beloved wife (in the theory's telling) Lyanna, and she died in childbirth -- mirroring the third, successful tempering of Lightbringer in the heart of a beloved wife.

spoken_quote: the sword in the darkness … the light that brings the dawn
paraphrase: Conclusion of the theory: Jon Snow is the forged weapon -- 'the sword in the darkness ... the light that brings the dawn' -- but the true hero, Azor Ahai reborn, is Rhaegar himself, who died years before the story even starts; each death (Rhaegar, Aegon, Lyanna) was necessary and predestined to save the world.

Speculative extension: maybe Rhaegar saw this outcome in dreams like his Targaryen ancestors did, and knew his fate was to die and to cause the deaths of everyone he loved -- which is why he could never be happy, never be truly known, or fully explain himself to anyone; that was his melancholy, his sense of doom.

The video ends on unresolved uncertainty: much remains unknown about Rhaegar's goals, the politics, the prophecy, and the heads of the dragon, and about Lyanna and Elia -- how did Elia feel about her husband running off with another woman, was she 'in on' the prophecy plan, and how much did Lyanna herself know?

The video notes that Lyanna, Elia, and Rhaegar's mother Rhaella are all women whose only narrative role is 'to be pretty, give birth, then die' -- almost nothing is known about their own perspectives on these events.
